content.text: Every interaction between an HTTP client and a server follows the same fundamental pattern: the client initiates a request, the server processes it, and the server returns a response — after which the connection either closes or is reused. For agentic AI systems, this cycle is the atomic unit of communication. Understanding every phase of a cycle — from DNS resolution through TLS negotiation, header delivery, body streaming, and connection reuse — turns vague latency problems into diagnosable, fixable bottlenecks.
What it is

The HTTP request/response cycle is the complete round trip that begins when a client sends an HTTP message and ends when the client finishes reading the server's reply. It is the basic interaction model of the World Wide Web and of virtually every REST, GraphQL, and streaming API used by modern software.

A cycle consists of: (1) a connection being established (or reused from a pool), (2) the client sending a request line, headers, and optionally a body, (3) the server receiving and processing that message, and (4) the server sending back a status line, headers, and optionally a response body. The body may be delivered all at once (buffered) or progressively as a stream of chunks.

HTTP is a stateless protocol: each cycle is self-contained. The server retains no memory of prior cycles unless the client re-supplies context on each request via headers, cookies, tokens, or a request body. This statelessness is both a strength (horizontal scalability) and a constraint (every request must carry its own authentication and context).
Why it matters

Agentic AI systems issue dozens to hundreds of HTTP cycles per task: calls to language model APIs, retrieval services, tool endpoints, webhook callbacks, and orchestration layers. The total latency a user experiences is dominated by how many of these cycles run in series, how long each one takes, and how efficiently connections are managed.

Two common agent performance problems both trace back to HTTP cycle misunderstanding. The first is unnecessary connection overhead: creating a new HTTP client on every tool call forces a new TCP handshake and, for HTTPS, a TLS handshake on every call — adding 50–300 ms of overhead that compounds across dozens of calls. The second is misconfigured timeouts: a single global timeout of five seconds will abort long-running LLM generations, while an unlimited read timeout will cause an agent worker to hang indefinitely when a remote API stalls.

Understanding the cycle also matters for streaming. When an LLM streams its response, the HTTP cycle stays open while the server sends Server-Sent Events (SSE) or chunked transfer chunks. The client must consume those chunks incrementally rather than buffering the entire response, or the streaming UX benefit disappears. Properly reading and forwarding streaming cycles is one of the most common requirements in agent server implementations.
Benefits
Ubiquity
— HTTP is supported by every language, runtime, and cloud provider. No custom protocol negotiation is required.
Statelessness enables horizontal scaling
— each request is independent, so agent workers can be replicated freely without shared session state.
Connection reuse (Keep-Alive)
— HTTP/1.1 keep-alive and HTTP/2 multiplexing allow many cycles to share a single underlying TCP connection, amortizing TLS cost.
Streaming support
— chunked transfer encoding and Server-Sent Events allow the server to push progressive content before the full body is ready, enabling token-by-token LLM output delivery.
Caching infrastructure
— HTTP's cache-control and ETag headers allow intermediate proxies and clients to short-circuit repeated cycles for idempotent resources.
Observability
— every cycle has a defined start and end, a status code, and measurable phases (DNS, connect, TLS, TTFB, transfer), making performance profiling straightforward.
Trade-offs
Head-of-line blocking (HTTP/1.1)
— HTTP/1.1 allows only one outstanding request per connection; parallel tool calls require multiple connections or HTTP/2 multiplexing.
Statelessness requires per-request context
— authentication tokens, session IDs, and agent context must be re-sent on every request; there is no implicit server-side conversation.
Long-lived streams hold connections open
— a streaming LLM response may keep a connection busy for 30–120 seconds, requiring heartbeat signals and careful timeout policies to prevent proxy or load-balancer timeouts.
No built-in push from server
— standard HTTP/1.1 is client-initiated; server-to-client push requires SSE, long-polling, WebSockets, or HTTP/2 push, each with different semantics.
TLS overhead on new connections
— each new TCP connection to an HTTPS endpoint pays a TLS handshake cost (one or two round trips). Connection pools eliminate this per-call.
Request/response mismatches in size
— large request bodies (file uploads, embeddings batches) and large response bodies (multi-turn context windows) both require care with timeouts and memory allocation.
How it works
The six phases of every HTTP cycle
Each HTTP exchange passes through a predictable sequence of phases. Understanding which phase is consuming time is the starting point for any performance investigation.
DNS resolution

— the client asks a resolver to convert the hostname (e.g., api.openai.com) to an IP address. On the first call this may cost 5–50 ms; subsequent calls hit the OS or resolver cache and are effectively free.
TCP connection

— the client performs a three-way handshake with the server IP on port 80 (HTTP) or 443 (HTTPS). This costs one network round trip (RTT). With a connection pool the TCP connection is already open and this phase is skipped.
TLS handshake

— for HTTPS the client and server negotiate cipher suites and exchange certificates. TLS 1.3 requires one RTT; TLS 1.2 requires two. Session resumption and 0-RTT tickets can eliminate this cost on reconnects.
Request transmission
— the client sends the request line (method + path + HTTP version), headers, and body. For a POST with a large JSON body, the transmission time depends on upload bandwidth.
Time to First Byte (TTFB)

— the interval between the last byte of the request leaving the client and the first byte of the response arriving. TTFB reflects server-side processing time — for an LLM API this is the model's prompt-processing latency.
Body download

— the client reads the response body. For a buffered JSON response this is a single transfer. For a streaming response the client reads chunks until the server signals completion (zero-length chunk in chunked transfer encoding, or a [DONE] SSE event).
HTTP versions and their impact on cycles

HTTP/1.1 serializes requests on a connection — only one in-flight at a time. To parallelize, clients open multiple connections (browsers cap at 6 per origin). HTTP/2 multiplexes many streams over one TCP connection, eliminating the need for multiple connections. HTTP/3 (QUIC) runs streams over UDP, eliminating TCP head-of-line blocking even at the transport layer. For agentic systems, HTTP/2 support in the client library is often the cheapest way to improve parallel tool call performance.
Connection pooling mechanics

A connection pool keeps TCP+TLS connections open after a cycle completes. On the next request to the same host, the pool assigns an idle connection, skipping phases 1–3. Pool exhaustion — when all connections are in use — causes callers to queue. For agent workers handling concurrent tasks, pool size should match the expected parallel in-flight requests per worker process. Under-sized pools serialize concurrent calls; over-sized pools exhaust file descriptors.
Streaming cycles and chunked transfer

When a server sets Transfer-Encoding: chunked (or Content-Type: text/event-stream for SSE), the HTTP cycle stays open and the server writes data incrementally. The client reads chunks as they arrive without waiting for the body to complete. This allows the client to begin processing — rendering tokens, routing partial results — before the full response is available. The cycle ends when the server sends a terminal chunk (zero-length chunk in chunked encoding) or closes the connection.
Architecture

The diagram below shows all six phases of an HTTPS request/response cycle between a client (browser or agent process) and a remote API endpoint. Phases 1–3 are one-time costs amortized across multiple requests when connection pooling is active.

attrs.alt: HTTP Request/Response Cycle — full phase breakdown showing DNS, TCP handshake, TLS handshake, request transmission, TTFB, and body/stream delivery

content.text: In a production agent system, the client is typically an agent worker process running inside a container. It maintains a persistent HTTP client that pools connections to the LLM API and to each tool service. Phases 1–3 only occur when a connection is first established or has been idle long enough to be evicted from the pool.
When to use it
Use a persistent HTTP client when
An agent loop makes repeated calls to the same API hostname (LLM, vector store, search API).
Agent workers run as long-lived processes (FastAPI workers, Celery workers) rather than short-lived serverless functions.
The API supports HTTP/2, enabling multiple parallel in-flight requests over one connection.
Round-trip latency is noticeable and DNS + TLS time is a measurable fraction of total call time.
Use streaming when
Displaying LLM output token-by-token in a UI or CLI reduces perceived latency significantly.
A downstream component (another agent step, a WebSocket client) can consume partial results before the full response is ready.
LLM generation time exceeds any reasonable non-streaming read timeout.
The response body is large and buffering it entirely in memory would be wasteful.
Do not use a shared persistent client when
The runtime is a short-lived function (Lambda, Cloud Run) where the client is created and destroyed on each invocation — there is no pool to reuse.
Each request targets a different hostname — pools are per-host and provide no benefit across hosts.
The framework (e.g., a test suite with mocked HTTP) makes persistent clients inconvenient and there is no performance requirement.
How to implement
Step 1 — Install a capable HTTP library

Python's built-in urllib is functional but does not support connection pooling or HTTP/2 easily. The httpx library (sync and async, HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2) and requests + urllib3 (sync, HTTP/1.1) are the dominant choices for agent code. For JavaScript/TypeScript, the native fetch API and the undici library both support connection reuse.
pip install httpx[http2]  # adds h2 dependency for HTTP/2 support
Step 2 — Create a shared client at process startup

Create one client instance per process (or per event loop for async) and reuse it for the lifetime of the worker. Pass base URL, default headers (authorization), and timeout configuration at construction time rather than per request.

import httpx
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager

# Sync client — one per worker process
http_client: httpx.Client | None = None

def get_client() -> httpx.Client:
    global http_client
    if http_client is None:
        http_client = httpx.Client(
            base_url="https://api.openai.com",
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {API_KEY}"},
            timeout=httpx.Timeout(
                connect=5.0,    # TCP+TLS must complete within 5 s
                write=10.0,     # request body upload
                read=300.0,     # long LLM generation
                pool=5.0,       # wait for pool slot
            ),
            http2=True,         # enable HTTP/2 multiplexing
            limits=httpx.Limits(
                max_connections=20,
                max_keepalive_connections=10,
            ),
        )
    return http_client

def shutdown_client() -> None:
    global http_client
    if http_client:
        http_client.close()
        http_client = None
Step 3 — Make requests through the shared client
Step 4 — Integrate with an application lifespan hook
In FastAPI or Starlette, use the lifespan context manager to ensure the client is created at startup and closed at shutdown. Never create clients inside request handlers.

from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from fastapi import FastAPI

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    get_client()           # warm up pool at startup
    yield
    shutdown_client()      # drain connections on shutdown

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)
Examples
Example 1 — synchronous tool call with connection pooling
This minimal example shows how connection reuse eliminates repeated TLS overhead across multiple sequential tool calls within an agent step.

import httpx
import time

BASE = "https://httpbin.org"

# Create client once — pool is established on first connect
with httpx.Client(base_url=BASE, timeout=10.0) as client:
    # First call: pays DNS + TCP + TLS (cold start)
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    r1 = client.get("/get", params={"call": 1})
    print(f"Call 1: {(time.perf_counter()-t0)*1000:.0f} ms  status={r1.status_code}")

    # Second call: reuses existing TCP+TLS connection
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    r2 = client.get("/get", params={"call": 2})
    print(f"Call 2: {(time.perf_counter()-t0)*1000:.0f} ms  status={r2.status_code}")
    # Call 2 is typically 5-20x faster because phases 1-3 are skipped
Example 2 — streaming SSE from an LLM API
This example demonstrates how to consume a Server-Sent Events stream from an OpenAI-compatible endpoint, processing each token chunk as it arrives without buffering the full response.
Example 3 — async parallel tool calls with httpx.AsyncClient

Agent systems often need to call multiple tool endpoints concurrently. AsyncClient with asyncio.gather issues all requests simultaneously, waiting only as long as the slowest response rather than summing all latencies.

import asyncio
import httpx


async def fetch_tool(client: httpx.AsyncClient, url: str, payload: dict) -> dict:
    resp = await client.post(url, json=payload)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()


async def parallel_tool_calls(tool_requests: list[tuple[str, dict]]) -> list[dict]:
    """Issue all tool calls concurrently; return results in original order."""
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=httpx.Timeout(connect=5.0, read=60.0)) as client:
        tasks = [fetch_tool(client, url, payload) for url, payload in tool_requests]
        return await asyncio.gather(*tasks)


# Example: three tool calls that would take 3s serial now take ~1s
tool_calls = [
    ("https://api.example.com/search", {"q": "agent architectures"}),
    ("https://api.example.com/weather", {"city": "London"}),
    ("https://api.example.com/stock", {"ticker": "NVDA"}),
]
results = asyncio.run(parallel_tool_calls(tool_calls))
Real-world example

Consider a production agent server that handles user requests by orchestrating calls to an LLM, a vector retrieval service, and a web search API. On each inbound request the server must: (1) embed the user query via an embeddings API, (2) retrieve relevant context from a vector store, (3) call the LLM with the assembled prompt, and (4) stream the LLM output back to the user via SSE.

import asyncio
import httpx
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse

EMBED_URL = "https://api.openai.com/v1/embeddings"
SEARCH_URL = "https://my-vector-store.example.com/search"
LLM_URL = "https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions"
HEADERS = {"Authorization": "Bearer sk-..."}

app = FastAPI()
_client: httpx.AsyncClient | None = None


def client() -> httpx.AsyncClient:
    return _client  # type: ignore


@app.on_event("startup")
async def startup():
    global _client
    _client = httpx.AsyncClient(
        headers=HEADERS,
        timeout=httpx.Timeout(connect=5.0, read=300.0),
        http2=True,
        limits=httpx.Limits(max_keepalive_connections=20),
    )


@app.on_event("shutdown")
async def shutdown():
    await _client.aclose()


@app.post("/chat")
async def chat(body: dict):
    user_query = body["message"]

    # Phase A: embed + retrieve run concurrently (parallel HTTP cycles)
    embed_task = client().post(EMBED_URL, json={"input": user_query, "model": "text-embedding-3-small"})
    embed_resp = await embed_task
    embed_resp.raise_for_status()
    vector = embed_resp.json()["data"][0]["embedding"]

    ctx_resp = await client().post(SEARCH_URL, json={"vector": vector, "top_k": 5})
    ctx_resp.raise_for_status()
    context_chunks = " ".join(h["text"] for h in ctx_resp.json()["hits"])

    # Phase B: stream LLM response back to caller
    async def generate():
        async with client().stream("POST", LLM_URL, json={
            "model": "gpt-4o",
            "messages": [
                {"role": "system", "content": f"Context: {context_chunks}"},
                {"role": "user",   "content": user_query},
            ],
            "stream": True,
        }) as llm_resp:
            llm_resp.raise_for_status()
            async for line in llm_resp.aiter_lines():
                if line.startswith("data: ") and line != "data: [DONE]":
                    yield f"{line}\n\n"

    return StreamingResponse(generate(), media_type="text/event-stream")

In this architecture every inbound request causes the agent server to act as an HTTP client. The shared AsyncClient pool means that for a busy server handling 20 concurrent user sessions, the 20 LLM connections share the same HTTP/2 multiplexed connection to the OpenAI API — reducing both connection overhead and the number of TLS sessions the server must maintain.
Common mistakes
Mistake 1 — Creating a new HTTP client per request
Mistake 2 — A single scalar timeout for all phases

# WRONG: 10-second total timeout aborts long LLM streaming calls
client = httpx.Client(timeout=10.0)

# CORRECT: separate limits per phase
client = httpx.Client(timeout=httpx.Timeout(
    connect=5.0,   # network dial should be fast
    write=15.0,    # allow large prompt body uploads
    read=300.0,    # LLM generation may take minutes
    pool=5.0,      # waiting for a free pool connection
))
Mistake 3 — Buffering a streaming response

# WRONG: reads entire body into memory before processing
resp = client.get(url)
body = resp.content     # blocks until transfer complete — defeats streaming

# CORRECT: iterate chunks as they arrive
with client.stream("GET", url) as resp:
    for chunk in resp.iter_bytes(chunk_size=4096):
        process(chunk)
Mistake 4 — Not calling raise_for_status()
HTTP 4xx and 5xx responses are not Python exceptions by default. Forgetting raise_for_status() causes agent code to silently process empty or error bodies as if they were valid responses.

resp = client.post("/v1/chat/completions", json=payload)
# WRONG: no error check — silently processes 429 or 500 body as success
return resp.json()["choices"][0]["message"]["content"]

# CORRECT
resp.raise_for_status()    # raises httpx.HTTPStatusError on 4xx/5xx
return resp.json()["choices"][0]["message"]["content"]
Mistake 5 — Not closing the client on process exit

Leaving an httpx.Client open at process exit causes resource leaks (open sockets, unclosed TLS sessions). Always close the client in a finally block, context manager, or application shutdown hook. In async code, await client.aclose().
Best practices
One shared client per process (or event loop)
— create it at startup, close it at shutdown, never inside request handlers.
Set all four timeout dimensions explicitly
— connect, write, read, and pool. Never rely on a single scalar timeout for agent workloads.
Use HTTP/2 when available
— httpx.Client(http2=True) adds multiplexing for free; it falls back gracefully to HTTP/1.1 for servers that do not support it.
Always call raise_for_status() before reading the body
— catch HTTP errors as exceptions, not as malformed JSON.
Stream when the body may be large or incremental
— use client.stream() context manager and iter_lines() or iter_bytes() to avoid buffering large payloads in memory.
Size the connection pool to match concurrency
— httpx.Limits(max_keepalive_connections=N) where N matches the maximum parallel requests a worker will have in flight.
Instrument TTFB separately from total cycle time
— TTFB reveals server processing latency; total time reveals transfer cost. Log both in agent traces.
Retry on transient failures with exponential backoff
— 429 Rate Limit and 503 Service Unavailable are expected; implement retry logic with jitter, not blind loops.
Verify TLS certificates
— never disable certificate verification (verify=False) in production; use a CA bundle or custom cert if talking to internal services.
Performance & scalability
Measuring cycle phases

The httpx library exposes timing via the elapsed attribute on responses, but only covers the interval between the first request byte sent and the last response byte received. For finer-grained profiling, wrap calls with perf_counter() around each phase or use OpenTelemetry instrumentation that records HTTP client spans with DNS, connect, and transfer sub-spans.

import time, httpx

def timed_call(client: httpx.Client, url: str) -> tuple[float, int]:
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    resp = client.get(url)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0
    return elapsed, len(resp.content)

# First call (cold): expect 200-400 ms for HTTPS with DNS
# Subsequent calls (warm pool): expect 20-80 ms for small payloads
HTTP/2 multiplexing for parallel tool calls

With HTTP/1.1 and a pool of size N, an agent can have at most N simultaneous in-flight requests to one host. HTTP/2 multiplexes many streams over one connection, allowing hundreds of in-flight requests without proportionally more connections. For agent systems that fan out to many tool endpoints on the same host (e.g., a vector store with multiple query types), switching to HTTP/2 can reduce connection count from N to 1 without changing application code.
Scaling connection pools across workers

Each worker process maintains its own connection pool. If an agent service runs 8 workers on one host, and each pool holds 10 connections to the LLM API, the host opens up to 80 connections to the API. Most APIs enforce per-key connection limits; coordinate pool sizes with rate limit tiers. Using a shared sidecar proxy (e.g., Envoy) with a single pool shared across workers avoids this multiplication.
Latency budget for a typical agentic turn
A rough latency model for a single agent turn that calls one retrieval tool and one LLM:

TTFB_embed  ~  50 ms   (embedding API, cached connection)
TTFB_search ~  30 ms   (vector store, cached connection)
[embed + search run in parallel → ~80 ms]

TTFB_llm    ~ 300 ms   (LLM prompt processing)
token_gen   ~ 500 ms   (50 tokens at 100 ms/token)
total_llm   ~ 800 ms

total_turn  ~ 880 ms   (parallel retrieval + sequential LLM)

Without connection pooling: add ~200-400 ms per cold HTTPS connection.
With serial retrieval (no parallelism): add ~80 ms extra.
Tooling & ecosystem
Python HTTP clients
httpx
— modern, sync + async, HTTP/2, timeouts per phase, built-in retry hooks. The recommended choice for new agent code.
requests + urllib3
— mature, sync only, HTTP/1.1. Wide ecosystem compatibility (boto3, many SDKs use it internally). Use when integrating with libraries that already depend on it.
aiohttp
— async-only, high performance, large ecosystem. Preferred in async codebases that prefer its streaming API style.
JavaScript / TypeScript HTTP clients
Fetch API (Node 18+)
— native, supports streaming via ReadableStream. No extra dependencies for basic agent code.
undici
— the underlying HTTP client for Node's fetch. Exposes connection pooling via Pool and Dispatcher directly.
axios
— browser + Node, popular in legacy codebases. Does not support HTTP/2.
Observability and debugging tools
curl -v / --http2
— inspect raw request/response headers and timing from the command line.
httpie
— human-friendly HTTP client for manual API testing.
Wireshark / tcpdump
— capture network packets to diagnose TCP/TLS timing at the byte level.
OpenTelemetry + httpx instrumentation
— auto-instrument HTTP client spans with trace context propagation across agent services.

# OpenTelemetry automatic instrumentation for httpx
from opentelemetry.instrumentation.httpx import HTTPXClientInstrumentor

HTTPXClientInstrumentor().instrument()
# All httpx.Client requests now emit spans with timing, status, URL
Comparison with alternatives
HTTP request/response is one of several communication patterns available to agent systems. The right choice depends on latency requirements, directionality, and connection lifetime.
Pattern
Directionality
Connection
Use in agents
Latency
HTTP request/response
Client-initiated
Per request or pooled
Tool calls, LLM APIs, webhooks
Low (warm pool)
WebSocket
Bidirectional
Persistent (full-duplex)
Real-time agent ↔ UI, live tool feeds
Very low (no per-msg overhead)
Server-Sent Events (SSE)
Server → client
Persistent (one direction)
Streaming LLM output to browser
Low (built on HTTP)
gRPC
Bidirectional streaming
HTTP/2 multiplexed
High-throughput internal microservices
Very low (binary Protobuf)
Message queue (AMQP/SQS)
Asynchronous
Broker-mediated
Decoupled agent steps, retries
Higher (broker hop)
Long-polling
Client-initiated loop
Repeated short connections
Legacy push simulation without SSE
Higher (polling overhead)

For most agent-to-API communication, HTTP request/response over a pooled connection is the right default. WebSocket or SSE is appropriate when the server needs to push events without a client request. gRPC suits high-throughput internal service meshes where Protobuf efficiency matters. Message queues suit asynchronous agent pipelines where steps are decoupled in time.
Security & reliability
TLS certificate verification

Always verify TLS certificates. Setting verify=False in development is tempting for self-signed certificates but is frequently left in place in production, exposing the agent to man-in-the-middle attacks. Use a custom CA bundle instead: httpx.Client(verify='/path/to/ca-bundle.crt').
Sensitive data in headers and URLs

API keys should be sent in the Authorization header, never in the URL (URLs appear in server logs and browser history). Do not log full request headers — mask or omit Authorization and X-Api-Key values in structured logs.
Retry with idempotency keys

Retrying non-idempotent POST requests (e.g., tool invocations that have side effects) without idempotency keys can cause duplicate actions. For critical operations, pass an Idempotency-Key header on the initial request and reuse the same key on retries. The server deduplicates based on the key.
Rate limiting and back-pressure

LLM APIs enforce per-key rate limits on tokens per minute (TPM) and requests per minute (RPM). An agent that bursts too many parallel calls will receive 429 responses. Implement a token bucket or leaky bucket in front of the HTTP client to smooth request rates. The tenacity library provides retry-with-backoff decorators well-suited to this pattern.
Timeout defense for agent workers

Without read timeouts, a stalled upstream API will hold an agent worker thread/task open indefinitely. In a worker pool with 8 threads, 8 stalled LLM calls exhaust all workers and block all subsequent requests. Always set read timeouts even when the expected generation time is long — 300–600 seconds covers all practical LLM response times while still protecting against infinite hangs.
Mental model

Think of each HTTP cycle as a phone call: you dial (DNS + TCP + TLS), your partner picks up (server ready), you speak your request, you wait while they think (TTFB), they speak their response (body transfer), and you either hang up (connection close) or stay on hold for the next call (keep-alive). Connection pooling is like having a dedicated phone line that stays open — you skip the dialing steps entirely and go straight to speaking.

Streaming is like a call where the other party starts answering before they have finished thinking — they send you words as they come to mind rather than composing the full sentence first. You start writing down what they say immediately rather than waiting for them to stop.

Parallel tool calls are like a conference call: instead of calling three people one by one (serial cycles), you call all three simultaneously (concurrent cycles) and the wall-clock time equals the slowest caller rather than the sum of all callers.
Key takeaways

An HTTP cycle has six measurable phases: DNS resolution, TCP connect, TLS handshake, request transmission, TTFB, and body download. Connection pooling eliminates phases 1–3 for all but the first request to a host.
Create one HTTP client per process (or event loop) and reuse it across all agent steps. Creating a new client per call adds 50–300 ms of TLS overhead on every request.
Configure four separate timeout dimensions — connect, write, read, and pool. A single scalar timeout is never appropriate for agent workloads where LLM reads may take minutes.
Streaming responses keep the HTTP cycle open and deliver body bytes incrementally. Consume them with iter_lines() or iter_bytes() rather than buffering the full body.
HTTP/2 multiplexes many requests over one TCP connection, enabling parallel tool calls without proportionally more connections. Enable it with httpx.Client(http2=True).
Always call raise_for_status() before reading the response body. Silent 4xx/5xx responses are a leading cause of hard-to-debug agent failures.
Retry 429 and 5xx responses with exponential backoff and identical idempotency keys to avoid duplicate side effects on non-idempotent operations.

Total agent turn latency is dominated by the sum of serial HTTP cycles. Parallelizing independent cycles (e.g., embed + retrieve) with asyncio.gather() is the highest-leverage latency optimization in most agents.
